verification_methodology: Confident-recall + WebSearch cross-check on 6 highest-uncertainty attributions (Marcus-Tardos venue, Park-Pham arxiv, Kelley-Meka arxiv, Campos-Griffiths-Morris-Sahasrabudhe arxiv, Ellenberg-Gijswijt arxiv+venue, Keevash designs arxiv). Marcus-Tardos venue corrected from Annals (recall) to JCTA Series A (verified).

paired.single_part_probe: What is the maximum number of edges in a triangle-free graph on n vertices?
paired.single_part_expected: floor(n^2/4)
paired.multi_part_probe: For triangle-free graphs on n vertices: (a) what is the maximum number of edges? (b) which graph(s) achieve the bound? (c) state the generalization to K_{r+1}-free graphs.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) floor(n^2/4); (b) the complete bipartite graph K_{floor(n/2),ceil(n/2)} (Turán graph T(n,2)); (c) Turán's theorem: max edges in K_{r+1}-free graph on n vertices is achieved by T(n,r), the balanced complete r-partite graph; edge count is (1 - 1/r)(n^2/2) + O(n).
paired.decomposition_target: verifies E007 wrapper recovers (b) and (c) when single-part (a) is answered correctly
paired.source: Mantel 1907 (Wiskundige Opgaven) for triangle case; Turán 1941 for general

paired.single_part_probe: State the Erdős-Ko-Rado theorem for k-element intersecting families on [n].
paired.single_part_expected: For n >= 2k, an intersecting family of k-subsets of [n] has size at most C(n-1, k-1).
paired.multi_part_probe: For k-element intersecting families on [n]: (a) state the EKR maximum bound; (b) describe the extremal families; (c) what changes when n < 2k?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) C(n-1, k-1) for n >= 2k; (b) extremal families are 'stars' — all k-sets containing a fixed element; for n > 2k stars are the unique extremal families up to relabeling; (c) for n < 2k every pair of k-subsets intersects automatically (pigeonhole), so the bound becomes trivial — all k-subsets form an intersecting family.
paired.decomposition_target: decomposes naturally into bound / structure / regime split
paired.source: Erdős, Ko, Rado 1961 (Quart. J. Math. Oxford 12)

paired.single_part_probe: What is the Kővári-Sós-Turán bound on the number of edges in a K_{s,t}-free bipartite graph with parts of size n?
paired.single_part_expected: O(n^{2 - 1/s}) for K_{s,t}-free with s <= t (assuming standard convention s minimal).
paired.multi_part_probe: For K_{s,t}-free bipartite graphs with parts of size n: (a) state the Kővári-Sós-Turán upper bound; (b) for which (s,t) is the bound known to be tight up to constants? (c) what is the implication for the Zarankiewicz problem?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) ex(n, K_{s,t}) = O(n^{2-1/s}); (b) tight for (2,2) via incidence graph of projective plane (n^{4/3}); tight for (3,3) up to constants (Brown 1966); tight for (s,t) when t > (s-1)! (Kollár-Rónyai-Szabó 1996); open in general; (c) Zarankiewicz function z(m,n;s,t) is bounded by the same exponent.
paired.decomposition_target: validates wrapper recovery of regime-tightness analysis
paired.source: Kővári, Sós, Turán 1954 (Colloq. Math. 3)

paired.single_part_probe: State the chromatic-threshold version of Turán's theorem (Erdős-Stone-Simonovits).
paired.single_part_expected: For any graph H with chromatic number chi(H) = r+1 >= 3, ex(n, H) = (1 - 1/r + o(1)) n^2 / 2.
paired.multi_part_probe: Erdős-Stone-Simonovits theorem: (a) state the asymptotic edge bound for an arbitrary graph H of chromatic number r+1; (b) why does the bound depend only on chromatic number? (c) what does it say (degenerate case) for bipartite H?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) ex(n,H) = (1 - 1/r + o(1)) n^2/2 where chi(H) = r+1; (b) any H of chromatic number r+1 sits inside a sufficiently-blown-up balanced complete (r+1)-partite graph by Ramsey/blow-up; (c) for bipartite H (chi=2) the formula gives o(n^2) — it is asymptotically uninformative; sharper polynomial-exponent bounds (Kővári-Sós-Turán) take over.
paired.decomposition_target: tests decomposition recovery on degenerate-case caveat
paired.source: Erdős-Stone 1946; Erdős-Simonovits 1966

paired.single_part_probe: What is the number of permutations of [n] avoiding the pattern 132?
paired.single_part_expected: C_n = (1/(n+1)) C(2n, n), the n-th Catalan number.
paired.multi_part_probe: Permutations of [n] avoiding a single pattern of length 3: (a) what is |Av(132)|? (b) is the count the same for all 3! = 6 patterns of length 3? (c) what is the smallest pattern length where the avoidance count becomes pattern-dependent?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) C_n (Catalan number); (b) yes — for all six patterns of length 3, |Av_n(sigma)| = C_n (Knuth 1968 for stack-sortable = Av(231); same for the others by symmetry/bijection); (c) length 4: the patterns 1234, 1342, 1432, 2413, 3142 are split into 3 Wilf-equivalence classes — Av(1342) and Av(1234) and Av(1324) have different counts.
paired.decomposition_target: decomposition wrapper must preserve length-3 vs length-4 phase change
paired.source: Knuth 'Art of Computer Programming' Vol 1 (1968) for stack-sortable; Stankova 1994 / Bona 1997 for length-4 split

paired.single_part_probe: What is the Stanley-Wilf limit for a pattern of length k (existence statement)?
paired.single_part_expected: For every permutation pattern sigma of length k, the limit L(sigma) = lim_{n -> infty} |Av_n(sigma)|^{1/n} exists and is finite.
paired.multi_part_probe: Stanley-Wilf conjecture: (a) state the existence claim; (b) name the proof and approach; (c) what is L(sigma) for sigma = 1342 explicitly?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) the limit |Av_n(sigma)|^{1/n} exists and is bounded as n -> infinity, i.e. |Av_n(sigma)| <= c^n for some c = c(sigma); (b) proven by Marcus and Tardos (2004) via excluded-permutation-matrices reformulation, using Klazar's reduction (2000) of Stanley-Wilf to Füredi-Hajnal; (c) L(1342) = 8 (Bona 1997, exact value).
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface specific exact value for sigma = 1342
paired.source: Marcus-Tardos 2004 (J. Combin. Theory Ser. A 107, 153-160); Bona 1997 (J. Combin. Theory Ser. A 80) for L(1342)=8

paired.single_part_probe: Define Wilf equivalence of permutation patterns.
paired.single_part_expected: Two patterns sigma, tau are Wilf-equivalent iff |Av_n(sigma)| = |Av_n(tau)| for all n.
paired.multi_part_probe: Wilf equivalence: (a) define it; (b) which length-4 patterns are Wilf-equivalent? (c) does Wilf equivalence respect symmetry-group action (reverse, complement, inverse)?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) |Av_n(sigma)| = |Av_n(tau)| for all n; (b) length-4 splits into 3 Wilf classes: {1234, 1243, 2143, 1432, 2134, 3214, 4321, 4312, 3412, 4231, 3421, 4132, 2341, 3124} - actually the 24 patterns split into 3 Wilf classes of sizes 7, 8, 9 once trivial symmetry is quotiented; the three nontrivial-class representatives are 1342 (class of size 7 mod trivial sym), 1234 (class of size 8), 1324 (class of size 9); (c) yes — the 8 trivial symmetries (D_4 action: reverse, complement, inverse, and compositions) always preserve Wilf class.
paired.decomposition_target: decomposition wrapper must surface the symmetry-group quotient correctly
paired.source: Stankova 1994; Backelin-West-Xin 2007 (J. Combin. Theory Ser. A) for the 1234-1432 Wilf equivalence chain

paired.single_part_probe: What are the necessary divisibility conditions for the existence of a Steiner triple system on n points?
paired.single_part_expected: n ≡ 1 or 3 (mod 6).
paired.multi_part_probe: Steiner triple systems STS(n): (a) state the necessary divisibility conditions; (b) are they sufficient? (c) what is the count of non-isomorphic STS(13)?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) n ≡ 1 or 3 (mod 6) (equivalently: 6 divides n(n-1)/3 and 3 divides n-1, etc.); (b) yes — Kirkman 1847 proved sufficiency (every n satisfying the condition admits an STS); (c) STS(13) has exactly 2 non-isomorphic systems (the projective plane PG(2,3)-derived system and one other).
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must produce small-n classification count
paired.source: Kirkman 1847; STS(13) classification is classical, see Colbourn-Dinitz Handbook of Combinatorial Designs

paired.single_part_probe: What is the necessary order condition for a Hadamard matrix?
paired.single_part_expected: Order n must be 1, 2, or a multiple of 4.
paired.multi_part_probe: Hadamard matrices: (a) state the necessary order condition; (b) what is the smallest open order at the time the Hadamard conjecture was last extensively verified to a frontier? (c) name two construction families.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) n in {1, 2} or n divisible by 4; (b) historically the smallest open multiple-of-4 has shifted: 668 was resolved by Kharaghani-Tayfeh-Rezaie 2005; subsequent advances pushed the frontier — exact current smallest-open requires literature check, do not fabricate; (c) Sylvester construction (orders 2^k via tensoring); Paley I (order p+1 for prime p ≡ 3 mod 4 via quadratic-residue circulant); Paley II, Williamson, Turyn are additional families.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must refuse-to-fabricate on smallest-open frontier; fabrication of a specific value is FM-08 failure
paired.source: Hadamard 1893 conjecture; Paley 1933; Williamson 1944; Kharaghani-Tayfeh-Rezaie 2005 for n=668

paired.single_part_probe: What was Keevash's 2014 breakthrough on combinatorial designs?
paired.single_part_expected: Keevash proved the existence conjecture for Steiner systems and more general t-designs whenever the natural divisibility conditions are satisfied (and n is sufficiently large).
paired.multi_part_probe: Keevash's existence-of-designs result: (a) state the result; (b) what was the prior status before 2014? (c) name a follow-up that gave a different proof.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) for fixed t, k and divisibility-satisfying n sufficiently large, there exists a Steiner system S(t, k, n) — and more generally Keevash's randomized algebraic construction proves existence of designs with the natural divisibility conditions; (b) prior to 2014, existence was known only for restricted (t,k) (Wilson's theorem for k=3 and graph designs; t=2 case largely resolved); the t-design existence question for t >= 3 had been open since Steiner 1853; (c) Glock, Kühn, Lo, Osthus (2016, arxiv 1611.06827) gave a purely combinatorial proof via iterative absorption.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must distinguish randomized-algebraic (Keevash) from absorption (GKLO) approach
paired.source: Keevash 2014 (arxiv 1401.3665); Glock-Kühn-Lo-Osthus 2016 (arxiv 1611.06827)

paired.single_part_probe: What is the threshold function for the connectivity of the Erdős-Rényi random graph G(n,p)?
paired.single_part_expected: p_c(n) = log(n) / n; sharper: G(n, (log n + c)/n) is connected with probability tending to e^{-e^{-c}}.
paired.multi_part_probe: Connectivity in G(n,p): (a) state the connectivity threshold; (b) state the sharper Erdős-Rényi-Bollobás result with the e^{-e^{-c}} double-exponential; (c) what is the threshold for the appearance of a giant component?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) sharp threshold at p = log(n)/n; (b) at p = (log n + c)/n with c constant, P(G(n,p) connected) -> exp(-e^{-c}) as n -> infinity; the same window controls disappearance of isolated vertices; (c) giant component appears at the much sparser threshold p = 1/n (the Erdős-Rényi phase transition); for p = c/n with c > 1 a giant component of linear size emerges almost surely, while for c < 1 all components are O(log n).
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must distinguish connectivity threshold (log n / n) from giant-component threshold (1/n)
paired.source: Erdős-Rényi 1959/1960 (Publ. Math. Debrecen / Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató)

paired.single_part_probe: State the (symmetric) Lovász Local Lemma.
paired.single_part_expected: If A_1, ..., A_n are events each with P(A_i) <= p and each A_i is mutually independent of all but at most d others, and if e * p * (d+1) <= 1, then P(no A_i occurs) > 0.
paired.multi_part_probe: Lovász Local Lemma: (a) state the symmetric version; (b) name the constructive (algorithmic) version that broke a long-standing barrier; (c) give one application class.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) e*p*(d+1) <= 1 implies P(intersection of complements) > 0 (Erdős-Lovász 1975); (b) the Moser-Tardos algorithm (2010, J. ACM) gives a constructive version that finds the avoiding configuration in expected polynomial time, removing the non-constructive barrier of the original probabilistic-method proof; (c) applications include k-SAT satisfiability for bounded clause-overlap, Ramsey-type lower bounds, hypergraph coloring (Beck), and many others.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface Moser-Tardos as the algorithmic upgrade
paired.source: Erdős-Lovász 1975; Moser-Tardos 2010 (J. ACM 57)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Kahn-Kalai expectation-threshold conjecture.
paired.single_part_expected: For any monotone increasing property F on a finite set, the threshold p_c(F) is at most O(p_E(F) * log(|critical-subgraph-count|)) times the expectation threshold, where the log factor was the conjectured tightness.
paired.multi_part_probe: Kahn-Kalai expectation-threshold conjecture: (a) state the conjecture; (b) what is its current status; (c) name one application of the result.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Kahn-Kalai (2007) conjectured that for every monotone increasing property F on the discrete cube, the actual threshold p_c(F) satisfies p_c(F) <= K * p_E(F) * log(L(F)) for an absolute constant K, where p_E is the expectation threshold and L(F) the size of the largest minimal element; (b) PROVEN by Park and Pham (2022, arxiv 2203.17207); short proofs followed (Tran-Vu 2023, arxiv 2303.02144); (c) implies the Shamir conjecture on perfect-matching thresholds in random hypergraphs and many threshold-tightness results in random combinatorial structures.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface the Park-Pham 2022 attribution
paired.source: Kahn-Kalai 2007 (Combin. Probab. Comput. 16); Park-Pham 2022 (arxiv 2203.17207)

paired.single_part_probe: What is the Cauchy-Davenport theorem for sumsets in Z/p?
paired.single_part_expected: For nonempty A, B subset of Z/p (p prime), |A+B| >= min(p, |A| + |B| - 1).
paired.multi_part_probe: Cauchy-Davenport and immediate generalizations: (a) state Cauchy-Davenport; (b) what is the Vosper theorem characterizing equality? (c) what does the general Plünnecke-Ruzsa inequality give for |A+A| vs |A-A|?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) for nonempty A, B in Z/p with p prime, |A+B| >= min(p, |A|+|B|-1); (b) Vosper 1956: equality |A+B| = |A|+|B|-1 with both >= 2 and sum < p forces A and B to be arithmetic progressions with the same common difference (modulo trivial cases); (c) Plünnecke-Ruzsa: if |A+A| <= K|A| then |kA - lA| <= K^{k+l} |A| for any k, l >= 0 — sumset growth is controlled across higher moments by the doubling constant.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface both Vosper structure and the Plünnecke iteration
paired.source: Cauchy 1813; Davenport 1935; Vosper 1956; Plünnecke 1970 / Ruzsa 1989

paired.single_part_probe: What is the maximum size of a cap set in F_3^n (the largest 3-AP-free subset)?
paired.single_part_expected: Bounded by O(2.756^n) by Croot-Lev-Pach / Ellenberg-Gijswijt; exact maximum known only for small n (n=3: 9; n=4: 20; n=5: 45; n=6: 112; n=7 lower bound 236, upper bound from CLP-EG).
paired.multi_part_probe: Cap set problem in F_3^n: (a) state the polynomial-method upper bound; (b) give known exact maxima for small n; (c) what is the lower-bound construction for n=7?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) at most c^n with c = 2.7559... (Ellenberg-Gijswijt 2017, arxiv 1605.09223; building on Croot-Lev-Pach 2017 for the F_4^n analogue); (b) n=1: 2, n=2: 4, n=3: 9, n=4: 20, n=5: 45, n=6: 112; (c) for n=7 the lower bound 236 is due to Edel (2004), constructed via lifting from the n=6 cap; the upper bound from EG gives ~1208, leaving a factor-5 gap.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface (a)+(b)+(c) without conflating CLP (F_4^n) with EG (F_3^n)
paired.source: Croot-Lev-Pach 2017 (Annals of Math 185, F_4^n); Ellenberg-Gijswijt 2017 (Annals of Math 185, arxiv 1605.09223, F_3^n); Edel 2004 for n=7

paired.single_part_probe: State the current best upper bound on the size of a 3-AP-free subset of [N].
paired.single_part_expected: Kelley-Meka (2023): |A| <= N / exp(C * (log N)^{1/12}) for some absolute constant C — superpolylogarithmic improvement on Behrend.
paired.multi_part_probe: 3-AP-free subsets of [N]: (a) state the Kelley-Meka 2023 upper bound; (b) state the Behrend lower bound; (c) what is the gap and what does it mean for Roth's theorem?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Kelley-Meka 2023 (arxiv 2302.05537): r_3(N) <= N * exp(-C * (log N)^{1/12}); (b) Behrend 1946: r_3(N) >= N * exp(-C * sqrt(log N)) by lifting from spheres; (c) the upper-bound exponent (log N)^{1/12} vs the lower-bound exponent (log N)^{1/2} = sqrt(log N) leaves a substantial gap; the Bloom-Sisask 2023 refinement (arxiv 2302.07211) sharpens the Kelley-Meka exponent slightly to (log N)^{1/9} via Bohr-set simplifications. Roth's theorem (any positive-density set contains 3-AP) is a consequence of any o(N) bound; current bounds quantify it.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must distinguish Kelley-Meka strong-bounds from Bloom-Sisask exposition/refinement
paired.source: Kelley-Meka 2023 (arxiv 2302.05537); Bloom-Sisask 2023 (arxiv 2302.07211); Behrend 1946 (Proc. Nat. Acad. Sci. 32)

paired.single_part_probe: State Freiman's theorem on sets with small doubling.
paired.single_part_expected: If A subset Z is finite with |A+A| <= K|A|, then A is contained in a generalized arithmetic progression of dimension d <= d(K) and size <= f(K) * |A|, where d(K) and f(K) depend only on K.
paired.multi_part_probe: Freiman's theorem: (a) state the structure theorem; (b) what is the best known dependence f(K), d(K)? (c) what is its analog in arbitrary abelian groups?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Freiman 1973: |A+A| <= K|A| forces A inside a d-dimensional GAP of size <= f(K)|A|; (b) Sanders 2012 quasi-polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa gave dimension d = O((log K)^{3+o(1)}) and size f(K) = exp((log K)^{3+o(1)}) |A|; the polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa conjecture (PFR) asks whether d = O(log K) and f(K) = exp(O(log K)) = poly(K); PFR over F_2^n was proven by Gowers-Green-Manners-Tao 2023 (arxiv 2311.05762); (c) the F_2^n version (subgroup analogue) is the Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa Conjecture; over arbitrary abelian groups Freiman has been generalized via Green-Ruzsa (2007).
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface PFR-over-F_2 settled status (Gowers-Green-Manners-Tao 2023)
paired.source: Freiman 1973; Sanders 2012 (Anal. PDE); Gowers-Green-Manners-Tao 2023 (arxiv 2311.05762)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Ramsey number R(3,3) and R(4,4).
paired.multi_part_probe: Diagonal Ramsey numbers: (a) state R(3,3), R(4,4); (b) what is known about R(5,5)? (c) state the best known asymptotic upper bound on R(k,k).
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) R(3,3) = 6 (classical); R(4,4) = 18 (Greenwood-Gleason 1955); (b) R(5,5) is open with current bounds 43 <= R(5,5) <= 46 (the upper bound was reduced from 48 to 46 by Angeltveit-McKay 2017, and the latest results may have moved it further by 2025-2026; verify before quoting a tighter exact value); (c) Campos-Griffiths-Morris-Sahasrabudhe 2023 (arxiv 2303.09521): R(k,k) <= (4 - epsilon)^k for an explicit epsilon = 2^{-7}, the first exponential improvement on the Erdős-Szekeres 1935 bound 4^k.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface CGMS 2023 attribution + refuse to fabricate exact R(5,5) value
paired.source: Greenwood-Gleason 1955 for R(4,4); Angeltveit-McKay 2017 for R(5,5)<=46; Campos-Griffiths-Morris-Sahasrabudhe 2023 (arxiv 2303.09521)

paired.single_part_probe: State Ramsey's theorem (finite version) and the key existence claim.
paired.single_part_expected: For all r, k_1, ..., k_r positive integers, there exists N = R(k_1, ..., k_r; r) such that any r-coloring of edges of K_N contains a monochromatic K_{k_i} in color i for some i.
paired.multi_part_probe: Ramsey's theorem (1930): (a) state the finite version with r colors; (b) state the infinite version; (c) what about hypergraph Ramsey?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) for r colors and target sizes k_1, ..., k_r, there exists finite N(r; k_1, ..., k_r) such that any r-edge-coloring of K_N has a color-i monochromatic K_{k_i} for some i; (b) infinite version: any r-coloring of edges of an infinite complete graph contains an infinite monochromatic complete subgraph; (c) hypergraph Ramsey R^{(t)}(k; r) on t-uniform hypergraphs: bounds grow tower-of-exponentials in k for t >= 3 (Erdős-Hajnal-Rado), and the lower bounds match the upper bounds at the tower level for t >= 4 (Erdős-Rado).
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must distinguish graph (t=2) from hypergraph (t>=3) Ramsey complexity classes
paired.source: Ramsey 1930; Erdős-Hajnal-Rado 1965 for hypergraph version

paired.single_part_probe: State the Erdős-Szekeres bound on R(k,k).
paired.multi_part_probe: Diagonal Ramsey upper bound: (a) state Erdős-Szekeres 1935 bound; (b) what was the next significant improvement? (c) is the lower bound 2^{k/2} (Erdős 1947) currently the best?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) R(k,k) <= C(2k-2, k-1) ~ 4^k / sqrt(k); (b) Conlon 2009 (Annals of Math): polylog improvement R(k,k) <= 4^k / k^{c log k}; the first exponential improvement came with Campos-Griffiths-Morris-Sahasrabudhe 2023 (arxiv 2303.09521) giving R(k,k) <= (4 - 2^{-7})^k; (c) lower bound: Erdős 1947 probabilistic argument gives R(k,k) >= 2^{k/2} (more precisely sqrt(2)^k / e); the 2024 Mattheus-Verstraete result improved off-diagonal Ramsey lower bounds, and improvements on the diagonal lower bound 2^{k/2} are very rare — sqrt(2)^k stood as the leading-order best until subsequent work (verify before quoting a 2024+ improvement).
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must distinguish polylog (Conlon) from exponential (CGMS) improvements
paired.source: Erdős-Szekeres 1935; Erdős 1947; Conlon 2009 (Annals of Math 170); Campos-Griffiths-Morris-Sahasrabudhe 2023

paired.single_part_probe: State the Hales-Jewett theorem.
paired.single_part_expected: For all r, k there exists N such that any r-coloring of [k]^N contains a monochromatic combinatorial line.
paired.multi_part_probe: Hales-Jewett theorem: (a) state it; (b) what is the density version due to Furstenberg-Katznelson; (c) what does it imply about Szemerédi-type density results?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Hales-Jewett 1963: for all r, k there exists HJ(r,k) such that any r-coloring of the cube [k]^N (N >= HJ(r,k)) contains a monochromatic combinatorial line; (b) Furstenberg-Katznelson 1991: density Hales-Jewett — for any delta > 0 and k there exists N(delta, k) such that any subset of [k]^N of density >= delta contains a combinatorial line; (c) DHJ implies Szemerédi's theorem (k-AP density theorem) directly via projection of [k]^N to integers; the Polymath 1 project (2010, with Tao et al.) gave a combinatorial proof of DHJ, replacing the original ergodic-theoretic argument.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface DHJ -> Szemerédi implication and Polymath-1 attribution
paired.source: Hales-Jewett 1963; Furstenberg-Katznelson 1991 (J. Anal. Math. 57); Polymath 1 2010

paired.single_part_probe: State Kuratowski's theorem on planar graphs.
paired.single_part_expected: A graph is planar iff it does not contain a subdivision of K_5 or K_{3,3}.
paired.multi_part_probe: Kuratowski / Wagner planarity: (a) state Kuratowski's theorem (subdivisions); (b) state Wagner's reformulation (minors); (c) what does this generalize to?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Kuratowski 1930: planar iff no subdivision of K_5 or K_{3,3}; (b) Wagner 1937: planar iff no K_5 or K_{3,3} as a graph minor; (c) generalizes via Robertson-Seymour Graph Minor Theorem: any minor-closed family of finite graphs is characterized by a finite list of forbidden minors (well-quasi-ordering of finite graphs under minor relation, proved across the 20-paper series 1983-2004).
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must distinguish subdivision (Kuratowski) from minor (Wagner) and surface RS GMT
paired.source: Kuratowski 1930; Wagner 1937; Robertson-Seymour Graph Minor series 1983-2004

paired.single_part_probe: State the Hadwiger conjecture.
paired.single_part_expected: Every graph with chromatic number k contains K_k as a minor.
paired.multi_part_probe: Hadwiger conjecture: (a) state it; (b) for which k is it known? (c) state the Robertson-Seymour-Thomas progress.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Hadwiger 1943: chi(G) >= k implies K_k is a minor of G; (b) trivial for k <= 3; k=4 equivalent to Four Color Theorem (proven Appel-Haken 1976, Robertson-Sanders-Seymour-Thomas 1997); k=5 proven (Wagner 1937 reduction to 4CT); k=6 open in general — Robertson-Seymour-Thomas 1993 reduced k=6 to 4CT under additional hypotheses; (c) for k >= 7 only weak results: Norin-Postle-Song 2019/2020 (arxiv 1910.09378) gave chi(G) <= O(t * (log t)^{0.354}) when G has no K_t minor — first improvement on the longstanding O(t * sqrt(log t)) of Kostochka 1984 / Thomason 1984.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface RST 1993 reduction + Norin-Postle-Song 2019 progress
paired.source: Hadwiger 1943; Wagner 1937; Robertson-Sanders-Seymour-Thomas 1997 for 4CT new proof; Norin-Postle-Song 2019/2020 (arxiv 1910.09378)

paired.single_part_probe: Define treewidth.
paired.single_part_expected: The treewidth of a graph G is the minimum, over all tree decompositions of G, of (max bag size - 1).
paired.multi_part_probe: Treewidth: (a) define treewidth via tree decompositions; (b) name two graph classes with bounded treewidth; (c) state the Courcelle theorem on monadic-second-order logic.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) a tree decomposition of G is a tree T whose nodes are labeled with subsets ('bags') of V(G) such that (i) every vertex is in some bag, (ii) for every edge uv, some bag contains both u and v, (iii) for every vertex v, the bags containing v form a connected subtree of T; treewidth tw(G) is min over decompositions of (max bag size - 1); (b) trees have treewidth 1; series-parallel graphs have treewidth <= 2; outerplanar graphs have treewidth <= 2; planar graphs do NOT have bounded treewidth; (c) Courcelle 1990: any graph property expressible in monadic-second-order logic (MSO) is decidable in linear time on graphs of bounded treewidth — a meta-algorithmic theorem covering many NP-hard problems on tree-like structures.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface Courcelle attribution + MSO-decidability framing
paired.source: Robertson-Seymour 1984 for treewidth definition; Courcelle 1990 (Inform. and Comput. 85)

paired.single_part_probe: What is the chromatic number of the plane (Hadwiger-Nelson problem)?
paired.single_part_expected: Currently bounded between 5 (de Grey 2018, Exoo-Ismailescu independent) and 7 (classical hexagonal-tiling upper bound).
paired.multi_part_probe: Hadwiger-Nelson chromatic number of the plane: (a) state the problem; (b) what was the historical bound and what changed in 2018? (c) what is the current best upper bound?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) chi(R^2) = minimum number of colors needed to color the plane so that no two points at distance exactly 1 share a color; (b) historically 4 <= chi(R^2) <= 7 from the 1950s (Nelson, Hadwiger), unchanged for 60+ years; in 2018 Aubrey de Grey constructed a 5-chromatic unit-distance graph (arxiv 1804.02385), pushing the lower bound to 5; subsequent Polymath 16 work refined the construction; (c) upper bound 7 from the regular hexagonal tiling with 7 colors (each hexagon colored, diameter < 1) remains best.
paired.decomposition_target: wrapper must surface de Grey 2018 lower-bound jump and refusal to claim above-7 upper bound
paired.source: de Grey 2018 (arxiv 1804.02385); classical 4 <= chi <= 7 unchanged 1950s-2017

trivial_case.description: k-uniform intersecting family on [n] with n >= 2k
trivial_case.result: max size is C(n-1, k-1) (the 'star' bound)

open_case.description: Frankl's union-closed conjecture: every union-closed family of sets contains an element in at least half the sets
open_case.result: best known constant approx 0.38197 (Sawin/Cambie/Chase-Lovett 2022, building on Gilmer)

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model conflates EKR (intersection) with union-closed (union closure) families OR gives 1/2 bound as proven
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: HARD-5: trivially-proven (EKR star bound) vs open (union-closed half-element) within adjacent set-system territory

trivial_case.description: Erdős-Rado sunflower lemma: family of k-sets with > (r-1)^k * k! sets contains a sunflower with r petals
trivial_case.result: (r-1)^k * k! upper bound

open_case.description: Erdős-Rado sunflower conjecture: c(r)^k bound (constant base instead of factorial)
open_case.result: Alweiss-Lovett-Wu-Zhang 2019 (arxiv 1908.08483) brought it to (C log k)^k for r=3; conjectured constant base

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model presents ALWZ bound as proving the conjecture, or attributes the original lemma to the modern improvers
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; trivially-proven lemma vs open conjecture within sunflower theory

trivial_case.description: Polynomial-method upper bound: every 3-AP-free subset of F_3^n has size at most c^n with c = 2.7559...
trivial_case.result: exponential improvement over earlier o(3^n / n) Bateman-Katz bound

open_case.description: Exact asymptotic constant c such that max cap-set in F_3^n is c^{n+o(n)}
open_case.result: current bounds 2.21^n (Edel lower-bound construction lift) <= max <= 2.7559^n
open_case.status: OPEN — gap of 0.5 in the exponent base

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims c = 2.7559 is the EXACT asymptotic, conflating upper bound with tight constant

trivial_case.description: Roth's theorem: every positive-density subset of N contains a 3-AP

open_case.description: Exact rate of decay of r_3(N)/N
open_case.result: Kelley-Meka 2023 (arxiv 2302.05537): r_3(N) <= N exp(-C(log N)^{1/12}); Behrend lower bound r_3(N) >= N exp(-C sqrt(log N)); gap from (log N)^{1/12} to (log N)^{1/2}

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Kelley-Meka or Bloom-Sisask 'solved Roth' (they sharpened the upper bound; the matching lower bound was already near-Behrend in 1946)

trivial_case.description: Szemerédi's theorem: every positive-density subset of N contains arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions
trivial_case.result: for every k, r_k(N) = o(N)

open_case.description: Exact rate of r_k(N) for k >= 4; specifically the polylogarithmic-Behrend-conjecture exponent
open_case.result: Bloom-Sisask, Leng-Sah-Sawhney 2024 made progress; the conjectured rate r_k(N) ~ N exp(-(log N)^c) for c depending on k is partially established
open_case.status: OPEN for tight constants when k >= 4

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model presents Szemerédi as 'fully solving' k-AP problem, ignoring the rate question

trivial_case.description: Hadamard matrices exist for all orders 2^k (Sylvester 1867)
trivial_case.result: explicit construction H_{2^k} = block recursion of H_1 = (1)

open_case.description: Hadamard conjecture: Hadamard matrix exists for every order divisible by 4
open_case.result: verified for all orders <= 664 and many others; smallest open frontier shifted with Kharaghani-Tayfeh-Rezaie 2005 resolving 668

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Hadamard conjecture is 'almost surely true' as if proven, OR attributes Hadamard to Sylvester (Sylvester gave the 2^k construction; the general conjecture is Hadamard 1893)

trivial_case.description: Specific small Ramsey numbers determined exactly
trivial_case.proven_by: various (Greenwood-Gleason 1955 for R(4,4); McKay-Radziszowski 1995 for R(4,5))

open_case.description: R(5,5) and beyond; asymptotic R(k,k)
open_case.result: R(5,5) bounded between 43 and 46 (Angeltveit-McKay 2017 upper); R(k,k) <= (4-2^{-7})^k (Campos et al. 2023 first exponential improvement on Erdős-Szekeres 4^k)
open_case.status: OPEN — R(5,5) exact value unknown for >70 years

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model fabricates specific R(5,5) value (42, 43, 45, 46 are all plausible-looking but R(5,5) is OPEN)

trivial_case.description: Stanley-Wilf conjecture: |Av_n(sigma)| <= c(sigma)^n for some c(sigma) finite
trivial_case.result: the Stanley-Wilf limit L(sigma) = lim |Av_n(sigma)|^{1/n} exists and is finite

open_case.description: Exact value of L(sigma) for general sigma of length k >= 4
open_case.result: exact: L(132)=4, L(1342)=8, L(1234)=9 (Regev 1981), L(1324) only known to be roughly in (10.27, 13.5) — exact value unknown
open_case.status: OPEN — L(1324) is the most famous open Stanley-Wilf limit

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims L(1324) has been computed (it has not — only bounds), OR conflates the existence-of-limit (proven) with exact-value (open for most patterns)

trivial_case.description: Erdős-Rényi connectivity threshold
trivial_case.result: G(n,p) is connected with high probability iff np - log n -> infinity

open_case.description: Shamir's perfect-matching threshold for random k-uniform hypergraphs (k >= 3)
open_case.result: Kahn 2023 (post-Park-Pham) confirmed Shamir's threshold conjecture for perfect matchings in random hypergraphs; before that it was known only via Park-Pham machinery
open_case.status: Recently CLOSED for many cases; some hypergraph-Shamir variants remain open

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model treats Shamir as still-open or as 'recently proven by Kahn-Kalai' (Kahn-Kalai gave the conjecture; Park-Pham 2022 proved the framework; Kahn 2023 + others closed specific cases)

trivial_case.description: Four Color Theorem: every planar graph is 4-colorable
trivial_case.result: chi(G) <= 4 for planar G
trivial_case.proven_by: Appel, Haken (1976; computer-assisted); Robertson-Sanders-Seymour-Thomas (1997 simplified proof)

open_case.description: Hadwiger conjecture for k >= 7: chi(G) >= k implies K_k is a minor of G
open_case.result: open for k >= 7; reduced to 4CT for k = 6 by Robertson-Seymour-Thomas 1993

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model treats Hadwiger as 'proven up to k=6' as if k=7+ is incremental; the structural barrier at k=7 is qualitatively different (no 4CT-style reduction known)

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Hadwiger-Nelson chromatic number of the plane

trivial_case.description: Bounds 4 <= chi(R^2) <= 7 from the 1950s
trivial_case.result: lower bound 4 from explicit unit-distance graph; upper bound 7 from hexagonal tiling

open_case.description: Exact value of chi(R^2)
open_case.result: de Grey 2018 (arxiv 1804.02385) raised lower bound to 5; Polymath 16 refined to small explicit graphs; upper bound 7 unchanged

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims chi(R^2) is known to be 7 (the upper bound) OR claims de Grey proved it equals 5 (de Grey raised the lower bound to 5; the upper bound remains 7)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; bounds-known vs exact-unknown; de Grey's contribution is lower-bound only

trivial_case.description: Robertson-Seymour Graph Minor Theorem: graphs are well-quasi-ordered under minor relation
trivial_case.result: every minor-closed family has a finite list of forbidden minors
trivial_case.venue: J. Combin. Theory Ser. B (multiple papers)

open_case.description: Polynomial-time recognition algorithms for specific minor-closed families with concrete constants
open_case.result: RS gave existence of O(n^3) recognition algorithms but with astronomical constants; Erdős-Pósa-style constants for specific families remain ongoing research
open_case.status: OPEN for many specific concrete-constant questions

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model presents RS GMT as 'gives polynomial-time algorithms for everything' without noting the constant explosion

trivial_case.description: Steiner triple systems STS(n) exist iff n ≡ 1 or 3 (mod 6)
trivial_case.result: necessary divisibility conditions are also sufficient

open_case.description: Existence of 1-factorizations of K_n for general perfect-1-factorization extensions; the Kotzig Perfect 1-Factorization Conjecture for K_n with n even and n >= 4
open_case.result: perfect 1-factorizations known for K_{p+1} (p prime) and many other orders, but conjectured to exist for ALL K_n with n even — open in general

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model conflates 1-factorization existence (trivially every K_n with n even has a 1-factorization) with perfect 1-factorization (the open conjecture)

trivial_case.description: Ramsey-Turán bound for triangle-free graphs of large independence number
trivial_case.result: for triangle-free graphs G on n vertices with alpha(G) = o(n), max edges is at most n^2 / 8 + o(n^2)

open_case.description: Ramsey-Turán density for K_4: max edges in K_4-free graph with alpha = o(n)
open_case.result: Erdős-Sós conjectured 1/8 * n^2; Sudakov 2003 confirmed upper bound matches lower bound for K_4 and certain other cases; full picture for K_t with t >= 5 remains open
open_case.status: OPEN for K_t with t >= 5 in some regimes

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model treats Ramsey-Turán as 'all done by Erdős-Sós', missing the K_t generalization gap

trivial_case.description: Erdős-Gallai theorem: graph G with average degree > k-2 contains every path on k vertices
trivial_case.result: tight bound for paths

open_case.description: Erdős-Sós conjecture: every graph with average degree > k-2 contains EVERY tree on k vertices (not just paths)
open_case.result: proven for sufficiently-large k by Ajtai, Komlós, Simonovits, Szemerédi (announced 2000s, manuscripts circulated; full publication delayed)
open_case.status: Resolved for large k; small-k cases reportedly open or only-recently-published

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Erdős-Sós is fully proven OR unaware of AKSS announcement
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; specific-tree (path) proven vs general-tree conjectured-and-mostly-proven

trivial_case.description: Off-diagonal Ramsey R(3, k)
trivial_case.result: R(3,k) = Theta(k^2 / log k) (Kim 1995 lower bound matching Ajtai-Komlós-Szemerédi 1980 upper bound)

open_case.description: Off-diagonal R(4, k) tight asymptotics
open_case.result: best lower bound R(4,k) >= Omega(k^{5/2}/log^2 k) (Mattheus-Verstraete 2024); upper bound R(4,k) <= O(k^3 / log^2 k) (Ajtai-Komlós-Szemerédi); gap of k^{1/2}

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims R(4,k) is k^3/log^2 k tight (it is only the upper bound) OR conflates the recent Mattheus-Verstraete improvement with closing the gap

trivial_case.description: Latin square of order n exists for every n >= 1
trivial_case.result: trivial: cyclic shift Z_n gives one; many others

open_case.description: Existence of three Mutually Orthogonal Latin Squares (MOLS(n)) for all n != 6 and n != 2; the related combinatorial-design existence-of-resolvable Steiner triple systems
open_case.result: Chowla-Erdős-Straus 1960 / Bose-Shrikhande-Parker 1959 resolved Euler's '36 officers' OMOLS(6) impossibility and existence for n != 2,6; the 3-MOLS existence for various n is open or recently resolved (Keevash-Sah-Sawhney 2022 type results)

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims '36 officers problem' is open (it is solved: no OMOLS(6) by Tarry 1900, Bose-Shrikhande-Parker 1959 for OMOLS existence n != 2, 6)

trivial_case.description: Freiman's theorem: small-doubling A in Z embeds in a generalized arithmetic progression of bounded dimension and size
trivial_case.result: bounded-doubling implies bounded-dimension GAP containment
trivial_case.venue: monograph 'Foundations of a Structural Theory of Set Addition'

open_case.description: Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa over Z: A with |A+A| <= K|A| is contained in a GAP of size <= poly(K) * |A| with dimension <= poly(log K)
open_case.result: Sanders 2012 quasi-polynomial; Gowers-Green-Manners-Tao 2023 (arxiv 2311.05762) proved PFR over F_2^n; PFR over Z is OPEN

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims PFR over Z is solved by GGMT 2023 (GGMT solved PFR over F_2^n; the integer version is still open)
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; F_2-version solved vs Z-version open

trivial_case.description: Zarankiewicz problem for K_{2,2}-free bipartite graphs
trivial_case.status: PROVEN tight up to constants for (2,2) and (3,3)

open_case.description: Zarankiewicz z(n,n; s, t) for general s, t
open_case.result: Kővári-Sós-Turán 1954 gives upper bound O(n^{2 - 1/s}); tight only for (2,2), (3,3), and (s,t) with t > (s-1)! (Kollár-Rónyai-Szabó 1996, Alon-Rónyai-Szabó 1999); general tight bound OPEN

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims KST bound is tight in general

trivial_case.description: Janson inequality for lower-tail of subgraph counts in G(n,p)
trivial_case.result: tight exponential lower-tail bound for subgraph indicators

open_case.description: Sharp upper-tail rate for triangle counts in G(n,p)
open_case.result: Lubetzky-Zhao 2017 / Harel-Mousset-Samotij 2019/2022 (arxiv 1904.08212) gave matching upper and lower bounds for the upper-tail rate function for triangles and many subgraph counts; some refined 'large deviation rate' questions remain technically open
open_case.status: Mostly resolved 2017-2022; some refined cases remain

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims upper-tail problem is wide open (it was resolved 2017-2022 for triangles and many subgraphs)

trivial_case.description: Hales-Jewett theorem (coloring version)
trivial_case.result: any r-coloring of [k]^N with N >= HJ(r,k) contains a monochromatic combinatorial line

open_case.description: Quantitative bounds on HJ(r,k) and density-Hales-Jewett bounds DHJ(k, delta)
open_case.result: Polymath 1 (2010) gave the first elementary combinatorial proof of DHJ; quantitative bounds remain weak (tower-type for k >= 3); polynomial bounds OPEN
open_case.status: Existence proven; sharp quantitative bounds OPEN

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Polymath 1 'proved DHJ' as if proving existence (Furstenberg-Katznelson 1991 already proved existence; Polymath gave the first elementary proof)

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Ryser / Brualdi conjecture on Latin square transversals

trivial_case.description: Existence of partial transversals in Latin squares of order n
trivial_case.result: Hatami-Shor 2008: every Latin square of order n has a partial transversal of size n - O(log^2 n)

open_case.description: Ryser's conjecture: every Latin square of odd order has a transversal
open_case.result: Montgomery-Pokrovskiy-Sudakov 2020 proved every Latin square has a transversal of size n - 1 for sufficiently large n; full Ryser conjecture (transversal of size n for odd order) and Brualdi-Stein conjecture (transversal of size n - 1 always) — Brualdi recently resolved; Ryser may have been resolved by Montgomery (announcement); verify before quoting closure
open_case.status: Recent progress; verify exact closure status

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Ryser is fully solved or fully open without acknowledging the recent rapid progress 2020-2024
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; partial-transversal vs full-transversal; recent-rapid-progress regime

trivial_case.description: EFL for small n
trivial_case.result: verified by Hindman 1981 and successors for n <= 10 and beyond computationally

open_case.description: EFL conjecture in full: union of n complete graphs each on n vertices, pairwise sharing at most one vertex, is n-colorable
open_case.result: Kang-Kelly-Kühn-Methuku-Osthus 2021 (arxiv 2101.04698) proved EFL for sufficiently large n
open_case.status: PROVEN for large n; small-to-large transition has unspecified threshold N_0

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims KKKMO 'proved EFL' without the 'sufficiently large n' caveat OR claims it remains entirely open
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; small-n-verified plus large-n-proved vs threshold-N_0-implicit

trivial_case.description: Vizing's theorem on chromatic index
trivial_case.result: chi'(G) is in {Delta(G), Delta(G)+1}

open_case.description: List-Coloring Conjecture (LCC): chi'_L(G) = chi'(G); equivalently, edge-list-chromatic number equals chromatic index
open_case.result: Galvin 1995 proved it for bipartite multigraphs; Kahn 2000 proved an asymptotic version with epsilon error; full LCC OPEN in general

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Galvin proved LCC in general (Galvin proved it for bipartite multigraphs)

trivial_case.description: Schur's theorem: any finite coloring of N has a monochromatic x + y = z
trivial_case.result: for any r-coloring of [N] with N >= S(r), there exist x, y, z with same color and x + y = z

open_case.description: Polynomial Schur / nonlinear Schur: monochromatic solutions to x + y = z^2 etc.
open_case.result: Frantzikinakis-Host 2018 and follow-ups gave density versions for some equations; specific equations like x + y = z^2 in N have density-monochromatic solutions; full polynomial-Schur OPEN

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims polynomial Schur is fully solved or that linear Schur covers it

attribution.probe: Who proved the Stanley-Wilf conjecture?
attribution.expected_answer: Adam Marcus and Gábor Tardos (2004), via reduction by Klazar (2000) of Stanley-Wilf to the Füredi-Hajnal conjecture on excluded permutation matrices.

attribution_tuples.venue: Journal of Combinatorial Theory Series A 107(1), pages 153-160
Formal Power Series and Algebraic Combinatorics conference / DMTCS

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Stanley alone (Stanley posed the conjecture; he did not prove it)
wrong venue (Annals of Mathematics is a common fabrication; correct is JCTA Series A)
wrong year (2002 or 2003 are common; correct is 2004)

attribution.probe: Who proved the polynomial-method bound on cap sets in F_3^n?
attribution.expected_answer: Jordan Ellenberg and Dion Gijswijt (2017) for F_3^n; the method came from Croot-Lev-Pach (2017) who proved it for F_4^n a few weeks earlier.

attribution_tuples.theorem: cap-set bound for F_3^n via polynomial method

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Tao (Tao popularized but did not prove)
attributing to Polymath (Polymath worked on related problems but the cap-set breakthrough was CLP and EG)
wrong year (2014 or 2016 common; the arxiv preprints are 2016 but published 2017)

attribution.probe: Who proved the existence of combinatorial designs (Steiner-type) for arbitrary t?
attribution.expected_answer: Peter Keevash (2014) proved existence of designs for sufficiently large n satisfying the natural divisibility conditions, using randomized algebraic constructions.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Existence of designs S(t, k, n) for sufficiently large n satisfying divisibility
Combinatorial proof of design existence via iterative absorption

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Wilson (Wilson 1975 proved existence for k=3 case and graph-design analogues; not the general t-design result)
attributing to Erdős (he posed many design questions; did not prove this)
wrong year (2010 or 2012 common)

attribution.probe: Who proved the Kahn-Kalai expectation-threshold conjecture?
attribution.expected_answer: Jinyoung Park and Huy Tuan Pham (2022).
common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Kahn-Kalai (they posed the conjecture; did not prove it)
attributing to Talagrand (Talagrand worked on related concentration; not this)
wrong year (2021 or 2023 common)

attribution.probe: Who proved the first exponential improvement on the diagonal Ramsey upper bound?
attribution.expected_answer: Marcelo Campos, Simon Griffiths, Robert Morris, and Julian Sahasrabudhe (2023): R(k,k) <= (4 - epsilon)^k with epsilon = 2^{-7}.

attribution_tuples.theorem: R(k,k) <= (4 - 2^{-7})^k, first exponential improvement on Erdős-Szekeres 4^k

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Conlon alone (Conlon 2009 gave a polylog improvement, not exponential)
missing one of the four authors (especially Sahasrabudhe is sometimes dropped)
wrong year (2024 common; arxiv submission was March 2023)

attribution.probe: Who proved the sunflower lemma improvement from k! to (log k)^k for r=3?
attribution.expected_answer: Ryan Alweiss, Shachar Lovett, Kewen Wu, and Jiapeng Zhang (2019/2020).

attribution_tuples.theorem: (C log k)^k bound on 3-sunflower-free k-uniform families

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Erdős-Ko-Rado (different theorem entirely)
missing Wu (the third author)
wrong year (2018 common; arxiv was August 2019)

attribution.probe: Who proved the recent strong bounds for sets free of 3-term arithmetic progressions in [N]?
attribution.expected_answer: Zander Kelley and Raghu Meka (2023): r_3(N) <= N exp(-C(log N)^{1/12}); Bloom-Sisask (2023) refined to (log N)^{1/9}.

attribution_tuples.theorem: r_3(N) <= N exp(-C(log N)^{1/12}) — strong bounds for 3-progressions
Refined bound r_3(N) <= N exp(-C(log N)^{1/9}) via Bohr-set simplification

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Bloom-Sisask alone (they refined; KM was the breakthrough)
attributing to Schoen (Schoen has earlier related work; not this)
wrong arxiv ID (2302.07211 is Bloom-Sisask; 2302.05537 is KM)

attribution.probe: Who proved Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa over F_2^n?
attribution.expected_answer: Tim Gowers, Ben Green, Freddie Manners, and Terence Tao (2023).
common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Sanders (Sanders 2012 proved the quasi-polynomial version, weaker)
claiming it solved PFR over Z (it solved over F_2^n; Z version is OPEN)
missing Manners (the third author, sometimes dropped)

attribution.probe: Who proved the 5-chromatic unit-distance graph (improving Hadwiger-Nelson lower bound)?
attribution.expected_answer: Aubrey de Grey (2018); Polymath 16 subsequently refined the construction to smaller graphs.
common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Polymath (Polymath 16 came after de Grey, refining)
claiming chi(R^2) was proven to be 5 exactly (de Grey only raised the LOWER bound; upper bound 7 stands)
wrong year (2017 or 2019 common)

attribution.probe: Who proved the Robertson-Seymour Graph Minor Theorem?
attribution.expected_answer: Neil Robertson and Paul Seymour, across the 20-paper Graph Minors series spanning 1983-2004.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Graph Minor Theorem: finite graphs are well-quasi-ordered under minor relation
attribution_tuples.venue: Journal of Combinatorial Theory Series B (Graph Minors I-XX)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Wagner (Wagner posed the conjecture, did not prove it)
claiming a single paper proves it (it is a 20-paper series)
wrong years (1990s common; the series began 1983)

attribution.probe: Who proved Mantel's theorem on triangle-free graphs?
attribution.expected_answer: Willem Mantel (1907); Pál Turán (1941) generalized to K_{r+1}-free graphs.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Max edges in triangle-free graph on n vertices is floor(n^2/4)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing only to Turán (Turán 1941 generalized; Mantel 1907 had the triangle case first)
wrong year for Mantel (1907 specifically)
wrong attribution to Erdős

attribution.probe: Who proved the Erdős-Ko-Rado theorem?
attribution.expected_answer: Paul Erdős, Chao Ko, and Richard Rado (1961). Note: the result was obtained earlier (around 1938) but published in 1961 due to wartime delays.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Erdős-Ko-Rado intersection theorem for k-uniform families

common_fabrication_patterns: wrong order of names (alphabetical: Erdős, Ko, Rado is correct)
wrong venue (Annals or J. Combin. Theory common fabrications)
missing Ko (Ko is sometimes dropped, leaving 'Erdős-Rado theorem' which is a different sunflower-related result)

attribution.probe: Who proved Szemerédi's theorem on arithmetic progressions in dense subsets of integers?
attribution.expected_answer: Endre Szemerédi (1975).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Szemerédi's theorem: every positive-density subset of N contains arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Roth (Roth 1953 proved the k=3 case; Szemerédi 1975 generalized)
wrong year (1973 common; correct 1975)
missing the multiple-proof history

attribution.probe: Who established the Behrend lower bound for 3-AP-free sets?
attribution.expected_answer: Felix Behrend (1946): r_3(N) >= N exp(-C sqrt(log N)) via lifting integers to spheres in high dimensions.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Behrend lower bound on 3-AP-free subset size
attribution_tuples.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 32

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Salem-Spencer (Salem-Spencer 1942 had a weaker construction; Behrend 1946 sharpened)
wrong exponent (often (log N)^{2/3} or (log N)^{1/3} appear as fabrications; correct is sqrt(log N))
wrong year

attribution.probe: Who proved the Lovász Local Lemma constructively?
attribution.expected_answer: Robin Moser and Gábor Tardos (2010): an algorithmic version finding the avoiding configuration in expected polynomial time.

attribution_tuples.venue: Infinite and Finite Sets (Colloq. Math. Soc. Janos Bolyai)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing only to Moser (Moser's earlier 2009 STOC paper handled the symmetric case; Moser-Tardos 2010 generalized)
wrong year (2009 sometimes; the JACM version is 2010)
venue confusion (STOC vs JACM)

attribution.probe: Who proved that K_{3,3}-free graphs have max edges Theta(n^{5/3}) up to constants?
attribution.expected_answer: W. G. Brown (1966) constructed dense K_{3,3}-free graphs achieving the Kővári-Sós-Turán O(n^{5/3}) upper bound.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Tight constant for Zarankiewicz z(n,n;3,3) ~ n^{5/3}

common_fabrication_patterns: wrong author for the construction (Erdős-Rényi, Reiman common fabrications; Brown specifically did K_{3,3})
wrong exponent

attribution.probe: Who proved the Four Color Theorem?
attribution.expected_answer: Kenneth Appel and Wolfgang Haken (1976) — first computer-assisted proof; Robertson, Sanders, Seymour, Thomas (1997) — simplified proof.

attribution_tuples.venue: Illinois Journal of Mathematics; Bull. AMS announcement

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing only to Appel or only to Haken (both authors)
wrong year (1977 common; the original announcement was 1976)
missing the simplified-proof attribution to RSST 1997

attribution.probe: Who proved Ramsey's theorem?
attribution.expected_answer: Frank Plumpton Ramsey (1930).
common_fabrication_patterns: wrong first name (Frank Plumpton Ramsey, not John Ramsey)
wrong year (1928 or 1929 common; the published paper is 1930)
wrong venue (J. London Math. Soc. as opposed to Proc. London Math. Soc.)

attribution.probe: Who established the first explicit Ramsey number R(3,3) = 6?
attribution.expected_answer: The result was known classically (Ramsey-style 'party problem'); a self-contained proof is in essentially every introductory combinatorics text. The R(4,4) = 18 result is due to Greenwood and Gleason (1955).
common_fabrication_patterns: attributing R(3,3) to a specific named person (it is folklore)
claiming R(3,3) was proven by Ramsey himself (Ramsey proved the existence theorem, not the specific value 6)

attribution.probe: Who proved the polylog improvement R(k,k) <= 4^k / k^{c log k}?
attribution.expected_answer: David Conlon (2009).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Conlon's polylog improvement on diagonal Ramsey

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Spencer (Spencer 1975 had earlier improvement on Erdős-Szekeres but smaller)
claiming Conlon's bound is exponential in k (it is polylog; the first exponential improvement is CGMS 2023)

attribution.probe: Who introduced the regularity lemma?
attribution.expected_answer: Endre Szemerédi (1975, in the proof of his arithmetic-progression theorem; published as a stand-alone in 1978).

attribution_tuples.venue: Problèmes combinatoires et théorie des graphes (1978 publication)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Erdős (Erdős had related ideas; the lemma is Szemerédi's)
claiming it was first stated in the AP-theorem paper alone (the stand-alone formulation came 1978)

attribution.probe: Who proved the Hadamard matrix of order 4n=668 exists?
attribution.expected_answer: Hadi Kharaghani and Behruz Tayfeh-Rezaie (2005), via Goethals-Seidel array constructions.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Hadamard matrix of order 668 (resolved smallest-open at the time)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Williamson or Turyn (these are construction-method names; the order 668 specifically is KTR)
wrong year (2003 or 2007 common)

attribution.probe: Who proved the Furstenberg-Katznelson density Hales-Jewett theorem?
attribution.expected_answer: Hillel Furstenberg and Yitzhak Katznelson (1991), via ergodic theory; Polymath 1 (2010) gave the first elementary combinatorial proof.

attribution_tuples.prover: Polymath (D.H.J. Polymath, including Tao, Gowers and others)

common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Furstenberg alone (Furstenberg-Katznelson)
claiming Polymath proved DHJ (they gave an elementary proof; FK had proved it 1991)
wrong year for Polymath

attribution.probe: Who proved Dilworth's theorem on partially ordered sets?
attribution.expected_answer: Robert Palmer Dilworth (1950).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Dilworth's theorem: in any finite poset, max antichain size equals min number of chains in a chain decomposition

common_fabrication_patterns: wrong first name (Robert Palmer, not Robert Henry)
wrong year (1948 or 1955 common)
attributing to Mirsky (Mirsky's theorem is the dual statement)

attribution.probe: Who proved Dirac's theorem on Hamiltonian cycles?
attribution.expected_answer: Gabriel Andrew Dirac (1952): every graph on n >= 3 vertices with min degree >= n/2 has a Hamiltonian cycle.
common_fabrication_patterns: wrong full name (Gabriel Andrew Dirac, not Paul Dirac the physicist)
attributing to Ore (Ore's theorem 1960 is a generalization)
wrong threshold (n/2 is correct; n/3 is fabrication)

attribution.probe: Who proved Cayley's formula for the number of labeled trees on n vertices?
attribution.expected_answer: Arthur Cayley (1889): the number of labeled trees on n vertices is n^{n-2}.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Cayley's formula: number of labeled trees on n vertices is n^{n-2}
attribution_tuples.venue: Quarterly Journal of Pure and Applied Mathematics 23

common_fabrication_patterns: wrong year (1857 or 1875 are early Cayley papers but not this result)
wrong formula (n^{n-1} or n!^{n-2} fabrications)
attributing to Prüfer (Prüfer 1918 gave a bijective proof; Cayley discovered the formula)

attribution.probe: Who proved the Bose-Shrikhande-Parker disproof of Euler's conjecture on orthogonal Latin squares?
attribution.expected_answer: Raj Chandra Bose, Sharadchandra Shankar Shrikhande, and Ernest Tilden Parker (1959-1960).

attribution_tuples.theorem: Existence of orthogonal Latin squares of order n for all n != 2 and n != 6 (refuting Euler's '36 officers' generalization)
attribution_tuples.venue: Canadian Journal of Mathematics; Proc. Nat. Acad. Sci.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Non-existence of OMOLS(6) (the 36 officers special case)
attribution_tuples.venue: Compt. Rend. Assoc. Franc. Avanc. Sci.

common_fabrication_patterns: missing one of the three names (especially Parker is sometimes dropped)
claiming Euler proved the 36 officers conjecture (Euler conjectured nonexistence for orders ≡ 2 mod 4; BSP disproved this for n >= 10)
wrong year

attribution.probe: Who proved the Erdős-Gallai degree sequence characterization of graphical sequences?
attribution.expected_answer: Paul Erdős and Tibor Gallai (1960): a non-increasing sequence d_1 >= d_2 >= ... >= d_n of nonnegative integers is graphical iff sum is even and certain inequalities hold for each k.
common_fabrication_patterns: attributing to Havel-Hakimi (their independent algorithm gives a different recursive characterization; Erdős-Gallai is the inequality form)
wrong year

attribution.probe: Who proved Vizing's theorem on chromatic index?
attribution.expected_answer: Vadim Vizing (1964): for any simple graph G, chromatic index chi'(G) is in {Delta(G), Delta(G)+1}.
common_fabrication_patterns: wrong first name (Vadim, not Viktor)
attributing to Gupta (Gupta independently proved a similar result, Vizing is the standard attribution)
wrong year (1965 common)

attribution.probe: Who proved the Galvin theorem on list edge coloring of bipartite multigraphs?
attribution.expected_answer: Fred Galvin (1995): the list-chromatic index of any bipartite multigraph equals its chromatic index.

attribution_tuples.theorem: Galvin's theorem (List Coloring Conjecture for bipartite multigraphs)
attribution_tuples.venue: Journal of Combinatorial Theory Series B 63

common_fabrication_patterns: claiming Galvin proved LCC in general (Galvin proved bipartite case)
attributing to Erdős-Rubin-Taylor (they posed the LCC; did not prove Galvin's case)
wrong year
